items.myth: A matrix is just any 2D array of numbers with no extra structure.
items.reality: While matrices look like 2D arrays, they come with specific algebraic operations (addition, scalar multiplication, transpose, and later multiplication) that must obey linear algebra rules.
items.example: In a plain 2D array you might elementwise multiply by another array of any shape using broadcasting, but in matrix algebra multiplication is not entrywise and has strict shape requirements.

items.myth: Matrix addition is always defined as long as both objects are matrices.
items.reality: Matrix addition is defined only when the two matrices have exactly the same shape. If their dimensions differ, A + B is undefined.
items.example: A 3 x 3 matrix of pixel intensities and a 2 x 3 matrix of features cannot be added even though both are matrices.

items.myth: The transpose does not change anything important; it is just a cosmetic operation.
items.reality: The transpose changes the shape and flips row and column roles. It turns row vectors into column vectors, swaps domain and codomain of linear maps, and is essential in defining symmetric matrices.
items.example: If w is a column vector (n x 1), then w^T is a row vector (1 x n). The scalar w^T x is defined when x is n x 1, but x w^T is an n x n matrix.

items.myth: Only square matrices are useful in machine learning.
items.reality: Rectangular matrices are extremely common, especially for datasets (m x n) and weight matrices between different layer sizes (n_in x n_out). Square matrices are special but not the only important case.
items.example: A fully connected layer mapping 128 features to 256 features uses a 128 x 256 weight matrix, which is not square but is central to the model.
